BIZ-OMICS · AQA A-Level Economics · Microeconomics

Price Determination in a Competitive Market

How demand and supply interact to set price, and how elastic those responses really are
Demand & Supply · Price, Income & Cross Elasticity · Price Elasticity of Supply · Market Equilibrium · Interrelated Markets

1

The Demand Curve & the Law of Demand

A demand curve shows the relationship between the price of a good and the quantity of that good consumers are willing and able to buy, holding all other influences on demand constant (ceteris paribus). Almost without exception, demand curves slope downward from left to right — a relationship known as the law of demand.

Demand
The quantity of a good or service that consumers are both willing and able to purchase at a given price, in a given time period — willingness alone is not enough; the consumer must also have the means to pay.
Law of Demand
As the price of a good rises, the quantity demanded of it falls, and as price falls, quantity demanded rises — producing a demand curve that slopes downward from left to right.
Fig 1: A downward-sloping demand curve. A fall in price from P₁ to P₂ causes a movement along the curve — an extension of demand from Q₁ to Q₂. A rise in price would cause the reverse: a contraction of demand.

Two distinct economic explanations underpin the law of demand — the diminishing marginal utility explored in the companion BIZ-OMICS Individual Economic Decision Making guide is one; the other rests on two effects that occur whenever price changes.

Income Effect
When the price of a good falls, a consumer's real income (purchasing power) effectively rises, since their fixed money income now buys more — this makes them able to buy more of the good (and other goods), increasing quantity demanded.
Substitution Effect
When the price of a good falls relative to substitute goods, the good becomes relatively cheaper and more attractive compared with its alternatives, causing consumers to switch towards it and away from now relatively more expensive substitutes, increasing quantity demanded.
Diminishing Marginal Utility
Because each additional unit of a good delivers less extra satisfaction than the last, consumers are only willing to pay a lower price to acquire further units — providing the utility-theory justification for the same downward-sloping shape (see the Individual Economic Decision Making guide, Section 5).
Why All Three Explanations Are Valid Together: AQA does not require you to choose only one explanation for the law of demand — the income effect, substitution effect, and diminishing marginal utility are complementary explanations that all point towards the same downward-sloping relationship, and the strongest exam answers reference more than one where space allows.
2

Movements Along vs Shifts of the Demand Curve

One of the most heavily tested distinctions in this entire topic is between a movement along the demand curve, caused only by a change in the good's own price, and a shift of the whole demand curve, caused by a change in any other factor affecting demand.

Extension of Demand
A movement along the demand curve caused by a fall in the good's own price, resulting in a rise in quantity demanded (Q₁→Q₂ in Fig. 1).
Contraction of Demand
A movement along the demand curve caused by a rise in the good's own price, resulting in a fall in quantity demanded.
Shift of the Demand Curve
A movement of the entire demand curve to a new position, caused by a change in any determinant of demand other than the good's own price — meaning a different quantity is now demanded at every possible price.
Fig 2: Shifts of the demand curve. A rise in demand shifts the whole curve rightward from D₁ to D₂ (more is demanded at every price); a fall in demand shifts it leftward to D₃ (less is demanded at every price). Neither shift is caused by a change in the good's own price.
Determinant (Non-Price)Effect of an IncreaseUK Example
IncomeDemand for normal goods rises (shift right); demand for inferior goods falls (shift left) — see Section 8.Rising real wages increase demand for restaurant meals out.
Price of substitutesA rise in a substitute's price increases demand for this good (shift right) — see Section 10.A rise in Netflix subscription prices can increase demand for Amazon Prime Video.
Price of complementsA rise in a complement's price decreases demand for this good (shift left) — see Section 10.A rise in petrol prices can reduce demand for cars.
Tastes and fashionA good becoming more fashionable or socially desirable shifts demand right.A celebrity endorsement boosting demand for a trainer brand.
Population and demographicsA larger or ageing population shifts demand for relevant goods right.An ageing UK population increasing demand for social care and mobility aids.
Advertising and brandingSuccessful advertising shifts demand right by changing consumer perception.A supermarket's Christmas advertising campaign boosting seasonal demand.
Expectations of future pricesExpecting prices to rise in future increases current demand (bringing purchases forward).Stockpiling ahead of an announced VAT rise.
Exam Trap to Avoid: never describe a change in the good's own price as "shifting the demand curve" — this is one of the most common errors at this level. A change in a good's own price always produces a movement along a fixed curve (extension or contraction); only a change in something else entirely produces a shift of the curve itself.
3

Price Elasticity of Demand: Definition & Calculation

Price elasticity of demand (PED) measures the responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in the good's own price — in other words, how much quantity demanded changes for a given percentage change in price, rather than simply the direction of the change already established by the law of demand.

Price Elasticity of Demand (PED)
A measure of the responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in price, calculated as the percentage change in quantity demanded divided by the percentage change in price.

The Formula

PED = % change in quantity demanded ÷ % change in price

Where % change = (change in the variable ÷ original value of the variable) × 100.

Worked Example 1 — An Elastic Good (Restaurant Meals)

The price of a restaurant meal rises from £20 to £25. As a result, weekly quantity demanded falls from 100 meals to 70 meals.

% change in price = (25 − 20) ÷ 20 × 100 = +25%
% change in quantity demanded = (70 − 100) ÷ 100 × 100 = −30%
PED = −30% ÷ 25% = −1.2

Key point: the negative sign confirms the law of demand (price and quantity demanded move in opposite directions) and is expected for virtually all normal goods; economists typically discuss the size (ignoring the sign) when describing elasticity, so this good is described as relatively elastic, since the percentage change in quantity demanded (30%) exceeds the percentage change in price (25%).

Worked Example 2 — An Inelastic Good (Prescription Insulin)

The price of insulin rises by 10%. Quantity demanded falls by only 2%, since patients cannot easily reduce their medically necessary consumption.

PED = −2% ÷ 10% = −0.2

Key point: quantity demanded barely responds to the price change at all — this good is relatively inelastic, since the percentage change in quantity demanded (2%) is much smaller than the percentage change in price (10%).

Worked Example 3 — Unit Elastic Demand

The price of a good rises by 10%. Quantity demanded falls by exactly 10%.

PED = −10% ÷ 10% = −1.0 (unit elastic)

Key point: when PED = −1 exactly, the percentage change in quantity demanded exactly matches the percentage change in price — this special case is explored further in Section 4 and Section 5.

Exam Tip on Method Marks: always show both percentage changes separately before dividing — AQA awards method marks for correctly calculating each percentage change even if the final division contains an arithmetic slip.
4

Interpreting Price Elasticity of Demand

Once a PED value has been calculated, it must be interpreted — AQA places heavy weight on being able to explain what a specific numerical value actually means for the good in question, not just on carrying out the calculation.

0
Inelastic (0 to −1)
Unit Elastic
Elastic (−1 to −∞)
−∞
Perfectly Inelastic (PED=0)Unit Elastic (PED=−1)Perfectly Elastic (PED=−∞)
PED ValueDescriptionMeaning
PED = 0Perfectly inelasticQuantity demanded does not change at all, whatever happens to price — a vertical demand curve.
0 > PED > −1Relatively inelasticQuantity demanded changes by a smaller percentage than price — consumers are not very responsive to price changes (Worked Example 2).
PED = −1Unit elasticQuantity demanded changes by exactly the same percentage as price (Worked Example 3) — total revenue remains unchanged whatever happens to price (Section 5).
PED < −1Relatively elasticQuantity demanded changes by a larger percentage than price — consumers are highly responsive to price changes (Worked Example 1).
PED = −∞Perfectly elasticConsumers will buy an unlimited quantity at one price only, and none at all above it — a horizontal demand curve.
Fig 3b: Elasticity is not constant along a single (non-linear) demand curve — it varies from point to point. Near the top left (high price, low quantity, such as the P₂/Q₂ point shown), a given price change produces a proportionately larger change in quantity relative to the small base quantity, so demand is elastic there; near the bottom right (low price, high quantity, such as P₁/Q₁), the same absolute price change is now proportionately larger relative to a lower base price, but produces only a small proportionate change in quantity, so demand is inelastic there. Only one point along the curve is exactly unit elastic.
Interpreting, Not Just Calculating: a full-mark answer never stops at stating the number — it explains what that number means in context. For example, a PED of −0.2 for insulin should be interpreted as: "demand is relatively price inelastic, meaning a 10% price rise causes only a 2% fall in quantity demanded, because patients have little choice but to continue purchasing a medically necessary good regardless of price."
5

PED and Firms' Total Revenue

Because PED measures how responsive quantity demanded is to price, it has a direct and predictable relationship with a firm's total revenue (also called total expenditure) — the total amount spent by consumers on the good, equal to price multiplied by quantity sold.

Total Revenue (Total Expenditure)
The total amount of money received by a firm from selling a good, calculated as price per unit multiplied by the quantity sold (TR = P × Q) — from the consumer's perspective, this is identical to total expenditure on the good.
PEDEffect of a Price RiseEffect of a Price Fall
Inelastic (0 to −1)Total revenue rises — the small percentage fall in quantity demanded is outweighed by the larger percentage rise in price.Total revenue falls — the small percentage rise in quantity demanded does not compensate for the larger percentage fall in price.
Unit Elastic (−1)Total revenue is unchanged — the percentage fall in quantity demanded exactly offsets the percentage rise in price.Total revenue is unchanged — the percentage rise in quantity demanded exactly offsets the percentage fall in price.
Elastic (beyond −1)Total revenue falls — the large percentage fall in quantity demanded outweighs the smaller percentage rise in price.Total revenue rises — the large percentage rise in quantity demanded outweighs the smaller percentage fall in price.

Worked Example — Revenue Effect of a Price Rise (Inelastic Good)

Using Worked Example 2 (Section 3): the price of insulin rises from £20 to £22 per unit (+10%), and quantity demanded falls from 1,000 to 980 units per week (−2%).

Original TR = £20 × 1,000 = £20,000
New TR = £22 × 980 = £21,560
Change in TR = +£1,560 (a rise, since demand is inelastic)

Key point: because PED is inelastic (−0.2), the firm's total revenue rises when it raises price, despite selling a slightly lower quantity — this is precisely why firms selling inelastic goods (from cigarettes to rail season tickets) have a strong incentive to raise prices.

Why Governments Use This Relationship for Taxation: because a price rise on an inelastically demanded good raises total revenue rather than reducing it substantially, governments frequently target inelastic goods such as tobacco, alcohol, and fuel with indirect taxes — the tax raises significant revenue precisely because quantity demanded barely falls, though this same inelasticity limits the tax's effectiveness in reducing consumption.
6

Factors Influencing Price Elasticity of Demand

Whether a good's PED is elastic or inelastic depends on a set of well-established factors, all of which affect how easily and how urgently consumers can change their behaviour in response to a price change.

FactorTends Towards Inelastic DemandTends Towards Elastic Demand
Availability of substitutesFew or no close substitutes (e.g. insulin, petrol in the short run).Many close substitutes available (e.g. one brand of bottled water versus another).
Necessity vs luxuryA necessity that consumers feel they cannot go without (e.g. basic groceries).A luxury that can easily be postponed or forgone (e.g. a foreign holiday).
Proportion of income spentTakes up a small proportion of income (e.g. salt, matches) — the price change is barely noticed.Takes up a large proportion of income (e.g. a car, a holiday) — the price change is highly noticeable.
Time periodThe short run, when consumers have not yet had time to find alternatives.The long run, when consumers have had time to seek out substitutes or change habits.
Habit-forming or addictive goodsAddictive goods such as tobacco or alcohol, where demand persists despite price rises.Non-habit-forming goods with no dependency effect.
Breadth of definitionA broadly defined good (e.g. "food" as a whole, with no substitute for food in general).A narrowly defined brand or specific good (e.g. one particular brand of breakfast cereal, easily substituted by another).
★ UK EXAMPLE: Rail Season Tickets vs Off-Peak Leisure Travel
Commuter rail season tickets tend to have relatively inelastic demand, since many commuters have no practical alternative way of reaching work at the required time and season tickets absorb a fixed necessary cost regardless of price rises — this is a major reason UK rail fares have historically risen faster than general inflation with limited loss of custom. By contrast, demand for off-peak leisure rail travel is far more elastic, since leisure travellers can more easily switch to coach travel, driving, or simply choose not to travel at all if prices rise.
7

Income Elasticity of Demand

Income elasticity of demand (YED) measures the responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in consumer income, rather than to a change in the good's own price.

Income Elasticity of Demand (YED)
A measure of the responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in consumer income, calculated as the percentage change in quantity demanded divided by the percentage change in income.

The Formula

YED = % change in quantity demanded ÷ % change in income

Worked Example 1 — A Normal (Luxury) Good: Restaurant Meals

Average household income rises by 8%. Demand for restaurant meals out rises by 12%.

YED = +12% ÷ +8% = +1.5

Key point: YED is positive and greater than 1 — demand rises by a larger percentage than income, identifying this as a normal, income-elastic (luxury) good (Section 8).

Worked Example 2 — An Inferior Good: Bus Travel

Average household income rises by 10%. Demand for local bus travel falls by 5%, as commuters switch to running their own car.

YED = −5% ÷ +10% = −0.5

Key point: YED is negative — demand falls as income rises, identifying bus travel here as an inferior good (Section 8).

Exam Tip: for YED, the sign (positive or negative) is the single most important piece of information, since it classifies the good as normal or inferior before any question of magnitude (luxury vs necessity) is even considered.
8

YED, Normal Goods & Inferior Goods

The sign and size of YED classifies every good into one of several categories, each with direct implications for how demand behaves as the economy grows or contracts.

YED ValueCategoryMeaningExample
YED < 0Inferior goodDemand falls as income rises — consumers switch to higher-quality alternatives as they can afford to.Value-brand supermarket ready meals; local bus travel (Worked Example 2).
0 < YED < 1Normal necessityDemand rises as income rises, but by a smaller percentage than income — spending on the good takes up a shrinking share of the budget as income grows.Basic groceries; household energy.
YED > 1Normal luxury (income elastic)Demand rises as income rises, by a larger percentage than income — spending on the good takes up a growing share of the budget as income grows.Restaurant meals (Worked Example 1); foreign holidays; premium electronics.
Fig 4b: Engel curves plot income (Y axis) against quantity demanded at each income level, rather than price against quantity. From common point e: a luxury good's curve (e→c) gets steeper as income rises, since demand grows faster than income (YED>1); a normal necessity (e→b) rises less steeply (0<YED<1); an inferior good's curve (e→a) slopes backward, since quantity demanded falls as income rises (YED<0) — the three cases in the table above shown on a single diagram.
Why This Matters for Business Strategy and the Economic Cycle: firms selling goods with high positive YED (luxuries) see sales boom during economic growth but suffer disproportionately during a recession, while firms selling goods with negative YED (inferior goods) can actually see sales rise during a downturn, as households trade down — understanding a product's YED is therefore central to forecasting sales across the economic cycle, a link explored further in the companion BIZ-OMICS Business Cycle guide.
9

Cross Elasticity of Demand

Cross elasticity of demand (XED) measures the responsiveness of quantity demanded for one good to a change in the price of a different good, capturing the relationship between goods that are substitutes for, or complements to, one another.

Cross Elasticity of Demand (XED)
A measure of the responsiveness of quantity demanded for good A to a change in the price of good B, calculated as the percentage change in quantity demanded of good A divided by the percentage change in price of good B.

The Formula

XED = % change in quantity demanded of Good A ÷ % change in price of Good B

Worked Example 1 — Substitutes: Tea and Coffee

The price of tea rises by 10%. As a result, demand for coffee rises by 4%, as some tea drinkers switch to coffee instead.

XED = +4% ÷ +10% = +0.4

Key point: a positive XED identifies tea and coffee as substitutes — as the price of one rises, demand for the other rises too. The relatively small size (+0.4, well below +1) suggests they are fairly weak or distant substitutes.

Worked Example 2 — Complements: Petrol and Cars

The price of petrol rises by 20%. As a result, demand for cars falls by 6%, as running costs deter some prospective car buyers.

XED = −6% ÷ +20% = −0.3

Key point: a negative XED identifies petrol and cars as complements — as the price of one rises, demand for the other falls, since the two goods are typically consumed together.

10

XED, Substitutes & Complements

As with YED, the sign of XED classifies the relationship between two goods, and the size of XED indicates how closely related they are.

XED ValueRelationshipInterpretation
XED > 0Substitutes (competitive demand — see Section 20)A rise in the price of Good B causes demand for Good A to rise, as consumers switch away from the now-more-expensive Good B. The larger the positive value, the closer the substitutes.
XED = 0Unrelated goodsA change in the price of Good B has no effect at all on demand for Good A — the two goods have no meaningful relationship.
XED < 0Complements (joint demand — see Section 20)A rise in the price of Good B causes demand for Good A to fall, since the two goods are consumed together. The larger the negative value (further from zero), the stronger the complementary relationship.
Fig 6: XED shown as a shift in demand. Left: a rise in the price of a substitute (e.g. tea) shifts the demand curve for the good in question (e.g. coffee) rightward from D to D′, since XED is positive. Right: a rise in the price of a complement (e.g. petrol) shifts demand for the good in question (e.g. cars) leftward from D to D′, since XED is negative. In both cases, nothing has changed in this good's own price — only its demand curve has shifted, exactly as Section 2 describes.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Strong vs Weak Substitutes
Two nearly identical supermarket own-brand baked bean products are extremely close substitutes, with a high positive XED — a small rise in the price of one causes a large swing in demand towards the other. By contrast, tea and coffee (Worked Example 1) are only weak or distant substitutes, since many consumers have a strong existing preference for one over the other regardless of relative price, producing a much smaller positive XED.
Exam Application: when a question describes a real-world price change in one market and asks about the effect on a related market, always identify the sign of XED first (substitute or complement) before discussing the size of the effect — this mirrors the approach for YED in Section 8 and is directly relevant to the interrelated markets covered in Section 20.
11

The Supply Curve & the Law of Supply

A supply curve shows the relationship between the price of a good and the quantity that firms are willing and able to supply to the market, holding all other influences on supply constant. Supply curves typically slope upward from left to right, a relationship known as the law of supply.

Supply
The quantity of a good or service that firms are both willing and able to offer for sale at a given price, in a given time period.
Law of Supply
As the price of a good rises, the quantity supplied of it rises, and as price falls, quantity supplied falls — producing a supply curve that slopes upward from left to right.
Fig 3: An upward-sloping supply curve. A rise in price from P₁ to P₂ causes a movement along the curve — an extension of supply from Q₁ to Q₂. A fall in price would cause a contraction of supply.
1
A higher price means higher potential profit per unit

Assuming costs of production stay constant, a rise in the price a firm can charge directly increases the profit margin available on each additional unit sold.

2
Higher potential profit provides the incentive to expand production

As profit-maximising, rational firms (see the Individual Economic Decision Making guide, Section 1), producers respond to this incentive by increasing output to capture the higher available profit.

3
This produces an upward-sloping supply curve

As price rises, the quantity firms are willing to supply also rises, generating the positive price–quantity relationship shown in Fig. 3.

The Incentive Function of Price: the law of supply exists because price acts as a direct signal and incentive to profit-motivated firms — a rational firm has no reason to expand output unless doing so is expected to be more profitable, and a price rise (holding costs constant) is precisely what makes expanded output more profitable.
12

Causes of Shifts in the Supply Curve

Just as with demand (Section 2), a change in the good's own price causes only a movement along a fixed supply curve. A shift of the entire supply curve is caused only by a change in a non-price determinant of supply.

Determinant (Non-Price)Effect of a Favourable ChangeUK Example
Costs of productionA fall in the cost of raw materials, wages, energy, or rent increases supply (shift right), since production becomes more profitable at every price.Falling global wheat prices reducing bakery input costs.
TechnologyAn improvement in production technology increases supply (shift right) by lowering costs per unit or raising output for the same input.Automation in car manufacturing lowering unit costs.
Indirect taxesAn increase in an indirect tax (e.g. VAT, a specific duty) decreases supply (shift left), as it raises the effective cost of supplying each unit.Increases in fuel duty raising the effective cost of supplying petrol.
SubsidiesA government subsidy increases supply (shift right), effectively lowering the firm's cost of production.Renewable energy subsidies increasing the supply of wind and solar power.
Number of firms in the marketNew firms entering the market increase total market supply (shift right).New entrants into the UK meal-kit delivery market increasing total market supply.
Weather and natural conditionsParticularly relevant to agricultural markets — favourable weather increases supply (shift right).A good harvest increasing UK wheat supply.
Expectations of future pricesFirms expecting prices to fall in future may increase current supply to sell now rather than later.Farmers selling stored grain ahead of an expected price fall.
Exam Trap to Avoid: exactly as with demand, never describe a change in the good's own price as a cause of a supply curve "shifting" — this always produces a movement along the existing curve. Only a genuine change in costs, technology, taxation, subsidy, firm numbers, or a similar non-price factor produces a shift of the whole curve.
13

Supply Under Perfect Competition: Supply = Marginal Cost

In a perfectly competitive market, an individual firm's supply curve is identical to its marginal cost (MC) curve above the point where price covers average variable cost — a result that links this topic directly to the theory of the firm and cost curves covered elsewhere on the specification.

Marginal Cost (MC)
The additional cost of producing one more unit of output.
Supply Curve = MC Curve (Perfect Competition)
Because a perfectly competitive firm is a "price taker" facing a given market price, it maximises profit by producing up to the output where price equals marginal cost (P = MC) — meaning the quantity it is willing to supply at any given price is read directly off its own marginal cost curve.
1
A perfectly competitive firm is a price taker

It has no power to influence the market price and must accept whatever price is set by the interaction of overall market demand and supply.

2
Profit is maximised where price equals marginal cost (P = MC)

A rational, profit-maximising firm keeps producing extra units as long as the price received exceeds the additional (marginal) cost of producing them, stopping exactly where price equals marginal cost.

3
Therefore the firm's MC curve shows how much it will supply at every price

At each possible market price, the profit-maximising output is read straight from the marginal cost curve — meaning the MC curve (above the shutdown point) doubles as the firm's individual supply curve.

Why This Matters: this result is precisely why a rise in costs (which shifts a firm's MC curve upward) shifts its supply curve left in Section 12's table — because the supply curve and the MC curve are, under perfect competition, one and the same line. This link is explored in full depth in the companion BIZ-OMICS Costs, Revenue & Profit and Perfect Competition guides.
14

Price Elasticity of Supply: Calculation & Interpretation

Price elasticity of supply (PES) measures the responsiveness of quantity supplied to a change in the good's own price.

Price Elasticity of Supply (PES)
A measure of the responsiveness of quantity supplied to a change in price, calculated as the percentage change in quantity supplied divided by the percentage change in price.

The Formula

PES = % change in quantity supplied ÷ % change in price

Worked Example 1 — Elastic Supply (A Manufactured Good With Spare Capacity)

The price of a manufactured good rises by 10%. Because the factory has significant spare capacity, quantity supplied rises by 25%.

PES = +25% ÷ +10% = +2.5

Key point: PES is positive (supply and price always move in the same direction under the law of supply) and greater than 1, identifying supply here as relatively elastic — firms can expand output substantially in response to a price rise.

Worked Example 2 — Inelastic Supply (A Perishable Agricultural Good)

The price of freshly harvested strawberries rises by 10% during the season. Because the current harvest is already fixed, quantity supplied rises by only 2%.

PES = +2% ÷ +10% = +0.2

Key point: PES is close to zero, identifying supply as highly inelastic — output cannot be increased quickly regardless of the price on offer, since the growing season fixes the quantity available in the short run.

PES ValueDescription
PES = 0Perfectly inelastic — quantity supplied is completely fixed, whatever happens to price (e.g. tickets to a sold-out one-off concert on the night itself).
0 < PES < 1Relatively inelastic — quantity supplied changes by a smaller percentage than price (Worked Example 2).
PES = 1Unit elastic — quantity supplied changes by exactly the same percentage as price. Graphically, any straight-line supply curve that passes through the origin has a PES of exactly 1 at every point along it, regardless of its slope.
PES > 1Relatively elastic — quantity supplied changes by a larger percentage than price (Worked Example 1).
PES = ∞Perfectly elastic — firms will supply an unlimited quantity at one price, and none at all below it (a horizontal supply curve).
Exam Tip on Unit Elasticity for Supply: unlike PED (where unit elasticity has a special total revenue property, Section 5), unit elastic supply (PES = 1) has a distinctive diagrammatic property instead: it is the only case where the supply curve, whatever its steepness, must pass exactly through the origin (0,0) if extended back. Recognising this straight-line-through-the-origin rule is a fast way to identify PES = 1 from a diagram without calculation.
15

Factors Influencing Price Elasticity of Supply

Whether a good's PES is elastic or inelastic depends chiefly on how easily and how quickly firms can adjust the quantity they produce in response to a price change.

Fig 10: The four benchmark supply curves. Perfectly elastic supply (horizontal) and perfectly inelastic supply (vertical) are the two extremes; between them, a flatter upward-sloping line indicates relatively elastic supply, while a steeper upward-sloping line indicates relatively inelastic supply — the steeper the curve, the smaller the percentage change in quantity supplied for a given percentage change in price.
FactorTends Towards Inelastic SupplyTends Towards Elastic Supply
Spare capacityFirms operating at or near full capacity, with no scope to raise output quickly.Firms with significant spare capacity, able to raise output with existing resources (Worked Example 1).
Stock (inventory) levelsLow stock levels, meaning firms cannot draw on stored output to meet a price rise.High stock levels, allowing firms to release stored goods quickly when price rises.
Time periodThe short run, when firms cannot vary all factors of production.The long run, when firms can vary all factors, including building new capacity.
Ease of factor mobilityFactors of production that are highly specialised and difficult to redeploy to this industry.Factors of production that can move easily between industries in response to price signals.
Gestation/production periodGoods with a long production or growing period, fixing supply in the short run (Worked Example 2).Goods that can be produced quickly once an order is placed.
Ease of storagePerishable goods that cannot be stockpiled ahead of a price rise.Durable, storable goods that can be held back and released when price rises.
★ UK EXAMPLE: Housing Supply Inelasticity
UK housing supply is well known to be highly price inelastic in the short run — planning permission, land availability, and construction time mean that even a substantial rise in house prices cannot be met with a rapid increase in the number of homes built. This inelasticity is frequently cited as a key structural reason why rising demand for UK housing (from population growth and low interest rates) has translated so strongly into rising prices rather than a proportionate rise in the quantity of homes supplied.
16

Market Equilibrium: How Demand & Supply Determine Price

In a competitive market, the equilibrium price is determined by the interaction of the market demand curve and the market supply curve — specifically, the single price at which the quantity consumers wish to buy exactly equals the quantity firms wish to sell.

Equilibrium
A state of balance in a market where quantity demanded exactly equals quantity supplied at the prevailing price, so that there is no inherent tendency for price to change further.
Equilibrium Price
The price at which the demand curve and supply curve intersect — the single price at which quantity demanded equals quantity supplied.
Equilibrium Quantity
The quantity bought and sold at the equilibrium price.
Fig 4: Market equilibrium. The equilibrium price (Pe) and equilibrium quantity (Qe) occur exactly where the market demand curve (D) and market supply curve (S) intersect at point e — the only price at which quantity demanded and quantity supplied are equal.
Why Equilibrium Is Self-Reinforcing: at the equilibrium price, both buyers and sellers are satisfied with the quantity being traded — there is no surplus of unsold goods pressuring price downward, and no shortage of unmet demand pressuring price upward — so, absent any change in a determinant of demand or supply, the market has no inherent tendency to move away from this price (see Section 17 for what happens away from equilibrium).
17

Disequilibrium: Excess Demand & Excess Supply

Any price other than the equilibrium price represents disequilibrium — a state in which quantity demanded and quantity supplied are not equal, creating automatic pressure that pushes price back towards equilibrium.

Disequilibrium
Any state of a market in which quantity demanded does not equal quantity supplied at the prevailing price, whether above or below the equilibrium price.
Excess Demand (Shortage)
A situation occurring when price is set below equilibrium, so that quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied — some consumers who are willing and able to buy at that price cannot obtain the good.
Excess Supply (Surplus)
A situation occurring when price is set above equilibrium, so that quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded — firms are left with unsold stock.
Fig 5: Disequilibrium and adjustment towards equilibrium. At P₁ (above equilibrium), quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded — a surplus that leads unsold-stock-holding firms to cut price back down towards Pe. At P₂ (below equilibrium), quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied — a shortage that allows firms to raise price back up towards Pe, as frustrated buyers bid price up.
1
Excess supply pushes price down

If price is set above equilibrium, firms are left holding unsold stock — competitive pressure to sell this surplus leads firms to cut prices, and as price falls, quantity demanded extends while quantity supplied contracts, closing the gap.

2
Excess demand pushes price up

If price is set below equilibrium, some willing buyers cannot obtain the good — this shortage allows firms to raise prices without losing all their custom, and as price rises, quantity demanded contracts while quantity supplied extends, closing the gap.

3
Price settles only at equilibrium

This adjustment process continues in either direction until quantity demanded exactly equals quantity supplied — the equilibrium price is the only price at which neither a surplus nor a shortage exists, and so the only price with no further built-in tendency to change.

18

Analysing Changes in Equilibrium Price

Because equilibrium price and quantity are determined by the intersection of demand and supply, any shift of either curve (Sections 2 and 12) changes where that intersection occurs — this is the standard diagrammatic technique AQA expects for analysing real-world market changes.

ChangeEffect on Equilibrium PriceEffect on Equilibrium QuantityReal-World Example
Demand increases (shifts right)RisesRisesA rise in incomes increasing demand for restaurant meals, raising both the market price and quantity of meals sold.
Demand decreases (shifts left)FallsFallsA health scare reducing demand for a food product, lowering both its price and the quantity sold.
Supply increases (shifts right)FallsRisesA bumper wheat harvest increasing supply, lowering bread prices while increasing the quantity sold.
Supply decreases (shifts left)RisesFallsA rise in fuel duty reducing petrol supply, raising the price while reducing the quantity sold.
Fig 7: A rightward shift of demand (D₁→D₂), supply held constant. The equilibrium moves from E₁ to E₂: both equilibrium price (P₁→P₂) and equilibrium quantity (Q₁→Q₂) rise — the first row of the table above shown as a full before-and-after diagram.
Fig 8: A rightward shift of supply (S₁→S₂), demand held constant. The equilibrium moves from E₁ to E₂: equilibrium price falls (P₁→P₂) while equilibrium quantity rises (Q₁→Q₂) — the third row of the table above shown as a full before-and-after diagram.
★ UK EXAMPLE: Energy Prices Following Russia's Invasion of Ukraine (2022)
A sharp reduction in the supply of natural gas to European markets following the 2022 invasion shifted the market supply curve for gas sharply to the left — with demand relatively unchanged in the short run (energy has few substitutes and low PED, Section 6), the new equilibrium settled at a substantially higher price with only a modest fall in quantity traded, precisely the "supply decreases" pattern in the table above, and a major driver of the UK cost-of-living crisis that followed.
Exam Technique for Diagram Questions: always follow four steps in order: (1) identify precisely which curve is affected and why; (2) draw the shift in the correct direction; (3) identify the new equilibrium point; (4) state clearly what happens to both equilibrium price and equilibrium quantity, using the vocabulary of Sections 2 and 12 (shift, not movement, since the change originates outside the good's own price).
19

Consumer & Producer Surplus

Consumer surplus and producer surplus together measure the total welfare a market generates for buyers and sellers at equilibrium — the essential foundation for the welfare-loss analysis used throughout the companion BIZ-OMICS Market Failure guide.

Consumer Surplus
The difference between what consumers are willing to pay for a good and what they actually pay — the extra value consumers gain from paying less than their maximum willingness to pay for every unit up to the quantity traded.
Producer Surplus
The difference between the price producers actually receive for a good and the minimum price they would have been willing to accept to supply it — the extra value producers gain from receiving more than their minimum acceptable price for every unit up to the quantity traded.
Fig 11: Consumer and producer surplus at equilibrium. Consumer surplus (green) is the area between the demand curve (D1) and the equilibrium price P1, up to Q1 — the value consumers gain from paying P1 rather than the higher prices they would have been willing to pay for earlier units. Producer surplus (orange) is the area between the equilibrium price P1 and the supply curve (S1), up to Q1 — the value producers gain from receiving P1 rather than the lower prices they would have accepted for earlier units. Total welfare (economic surplus) is the sum of both areas.

Worked Example — Calculating Consumer and Producer Surplus

A market has equilibrium price £6 and equilibrium quantity 400 units. The demand curve's vertical intercept (the maximum price the most eager buyer would pay) is £14. The supply curve's vertical intercept (the minimum price the least willing seller would accept) is £2.

Consumer surplus = ½ × base × height = ½ × Qe × (Pmax − Pe)
CS = ½ × 400 × (£14 − £6) = ½ × 400 × £8 = £1,600

Producer surplus = ½ × base × height = ½ × Qe × (Pe − Pmin)
PS = ½ × 400 × (£6 − £2) = ½ × 400 × £4 = £800

Total welfare (economic surplus) = CS + PS = £1,600 + £800 = £2,400

Key point: both areas are right-angled triangles, so each is calculated using the standard triangle-area formula (½ × base × height), with quantity as the base and the relevant price gap as the height — this is exactly the method needed to calculate the welfare-loss triangles used throughout the Market Failure guide's externality and tax/subsidy diagrams.

How Consumer and Producer Surplus Change

Because CS and PS are defined relative to the equilibrium price and quantity, any shift in demand or supply (Sections 2, 12, and 18) changes both areas — though not always in the same direction or by the same amount.

ChangeEffect on Consumer SurplusEffect on Producer Surplus
Demand increases (shifts right)Ambiguous in principle (price rises, which reduces the gain per unit, but quantity traded also rises) — in practice, CS usually rises, since the gain from substantially more units traded normally outweighs the smaller per-unit gap.Rises unambiguously — producers receive a higher price for a larger quantity.
Demand decreases (shifts left)Usually falls — both a lower price gap per unit and fewer units traded work in the same direction.Falls unambiguously — a lower price for a smaller quantity.
Supply increases (shifts right)Rises unambiguously — consumers pay a lower price for a larger quantity.Ambiguous in principle (price falls, reducing the gain per unit, but quantity rises) — in practice, PS usually rises if the quantity effect dominates.
Supply decreases (shifts left)Falls unambiguously — a higher price for a smaller quantity.Usually falls — both a smaller price gap per unit and fewer units traded work in the same direction.
Why the "Own Side" Effect Is Always Unambiguous: a shift in supply always has an unambiguous effect on consumer surplus, and a shift in demand always has an unambiguous effect on producer surplus, because the side of the market that didn't shift experiences both the price change and the quantity change working in the same direction. The side that did shift faces a genuine trade-off between a changed price and a changed quantity, which is why economists are more cautious asserting a definite direction for that side without knowing the relative steepness (elasticity, Sections 4 and 15) of the two curves.
Elasticity and the Size of Surplus Triangles: for a given equilibrium price and quantity, steeper (more inelastic) curves produce larger surplus triangles than flatter (more elastic) curves, because the vertical distance back to the axis intercept is greater — a good with very inelastic demand (e.g. insulin, Section 4) generates a large consumer surplus relative to its market size, since at least some buyers would have been willing to pay very much more than the equilibrium price.
Cross-Reference to Market Failure: the companion BIZ-OMICS Market Failure guide uses this same CS/PS framework throughout — an indirect tax (Market Failure guide, Fig. 7) reduces both consumer and producer surplus relative to the free-market equilibrium, with the "missing" surplus split between government tax revenue and a genuine deadweight welfare loss; a price ceiling or floor (Market Failure guide, Figs. 9–10) similarly redistributes and destroys surplus by preventing the market from reaching its equilibrium quantity. Every welfare-loss triangle in that guide is, at its core, an application of the CS/PS concept introduced here.
20

Assumptions of the Demand & Supply Model

The basic demand and supply model is a powerful and widely applicable tool, but like any economic model (see the BIZ-OMICS Economic Methodology guide) it rests on simplifying assumptions that should be recognised, particularly for higher-mark evaluation questions.

Ceteris Paribus
Each curve is drawn holding all other influences on demand or supply constant — in reality, several determinants often change simultaneously, making real-world price changes harder to attribute to a single cause than the model suggests.
Rational, Utility-Maximising Consumers and Profit-Maximising Firms
The model assumes consumers and firms behave as the rational agents described in Section 1 of the Individual Economic Decision Making guide — an assumption behavioural economics shows is only an approximation of real decision-making.
Perfect Information
The basic model assumes buyers and sellers know all relevant prices and product information — in practice, imperfect and asymmetric information (see the Individual Economic Decision Making guide, Section 11) can prevent the market from clearing as smoothly as the model predicts.
Homogeneous Goods
The model typically assumes all units of the good are identical, so that a single price and a single demand/supply curve is meaningful — in reality, branding and product differentiation mean many "single markets" are really collections of closely related but distinct products.
Instantaneous Adjustment
The model implies price adjusts smoothly and immediately to clear any surplus or shortage — in reality, prices can be "sticky" (slow to adjust) due to menu costs, contracts, or firms' reluctance to change prices frequently.
Exam Application: a strong evaluative answer does not reject the demand and supply model outright, but recognises it as a simplified, generally reliable starting point whose predictions may be less precise wherever these assumptions are most badly violated — for example, markets with highly differentiated products, significant informational asymmetries, or considerable price stickiness.
21

The Interrelationship Between Markets

Markets rarely operate in complete isolation from one another. A change in one market frequently has knock-on effects on other, related markets — captured by four specific concepts on the specification: joint demand, competitive demand, composite demand, and joint supply.

Joint Demand (Complementary Demand)
Occurs when two goods are demanded together, because they are typically consumed jointly — a rise in demand (or price) for one directly affects demand for the other, corresponding to a negative XED (Section 10).
Competitive Demand (Substitute Demand)
Occurs when two goods compete for the same consumer spending because they can be used in place of one another — a rise in the price of one increases demand for the other, corresponding to a positive XED (Section 10).
Composite Demand
Occurs when a single good is demanded for two or more distinct, competing uses — a rise in demand for one use reduces the quantity available for the other use(s), since total supply must be shared between them.
Derived Demand
Occurs when demand for one good (typically a factor of production or intermediate good) arises only because of demand for a separate final good it helps to produce — demand for the factor rises and falls in line with demand for the final product.
Joint Supply
Occurs when producing one good simultaneously and unavoidably produces another good as a by-product — an increase in supply of one automatically increases supply of the other, since they come from the same production process.
ConceptUK / Real-World ExampleEffect Traced Through
Joint DemandPrinters and printer ink cartridges.A rise in demand for home printers directly increases demand for compatible ink cartridges, even though ink's own price is unchanged.
Competitive DemandButter and margarine.A rise in the price of butter (e.g. due to a rise in milk prices) increases demand for margarine as consumers substitute towards it.
Composite DemandMilk demanded both for direct drinking and as an input into cheese and butter production.A surge in demand for cheese (composite use 1) reduces the quantity of raw milk available for direct retail sale (composite use 2) at the existing price, tending to raise the price of retail milk too.
Derived DemandSteel and new car production.A fall in demand for new cars (the final good) directly reduces demand for steel (the input), even though nothing has changed in the steel market itself.
Joint SupplyCrude oil refining, which simultaneously yields petrol, diesel, and other refined products from the same barrel.A rise in demand for diesel (encouraging refiners to process more crude oil) simultaneously increases the supply of petrol, even without any separate change in petrol's own market.
★ UK EXAMPLE: Joint Supply in Livestock Farming
Cattle farming is a classic case of joint supply — a single animal simultaneously yields beef (for the meat market) and hide (for the leather market) as an unavoidable joint product. If beef prices rise, encouraging farmers to raise more cattle, the supply of leather rises automatically alongside it as a direct consequence, even though nothing has changed in the leather market's own demand or price.
Fig 9: Joint supply between mutton and wool, both produced from the same sheep. A rise in demand for mutton (D₁→D₂ in panel a) raises the equilibrium price and quantity of mutton (E→F), which encourages farmers to keep more sheep — and because each sheep also yields wool, this automatically increases the supply of wool (S₁→S₂ in panel b), lowering wool's equilibrium price even though nothing has changed in the wool market's own demand.
Exam Application: when a question describes a change in one market and asks about the impact "on other markets," always identify precisely which of these four relationships applies (joint demand, competitive demand, composite demand, or joint supply) before analysing the direction of the knock-on effect — correctly naming the relationship is usually itself worth marks, separate from correctly analysing its consequences.
22

Exam Technique & Model Chains of Reasoning

The Golden Chain: From a Cost Shock to a New Equilibrium

A rise in the cost of a key input (e.g. a rise in global gas prices) raises firms' marginal costs of production → because supply equals the marginal cost curve under perfect competition (Section 13), the market supply curve shifts left → at the original price, quantity supplied now falls short of quantity demanded, creating excess demand (a shortage) → this shortage pushes the price upward → the market settles at a new, higher equilibrium price with a lower equilibrium quantity traded, exactly as Section 18's supply-decrease row predicts.

The Golden Chain: PED, Total Revenue and Tax Policy

A good has few close substitutes and is not a large proportion of income (Section 6), so its demand is price inelastic → the government imposes an indirect tax on the good, shifting supply left and raising the equilibrium price → because demand is inelastic, the percentage fall in quantity demanded is smaller than the percentage rise in price → total revenue (now largely accruing to the government as tax revenue plus firm revenue) rises rather than falls (Section 5) → this is precisely why governments target inelastically demanded goods such as tobacco and fuel for indirect taxation, even though the same inelasticity limits the tax's effectiveness at reducing consumption.

Key Exam Tips

1

Always distinguish a movement along a curve (caused only by the good's own price) from a shift of the curve (caused by any other determinant) — for both demand and supply, this single distinction underpins almost every diagram question in this topic.

2

For every elasticity calculation, always show both percentage changes as separate steps before dividing, and always state whether the good is elastic, inelastic, or unit elastic in words, not just as a number.

3

Remember the sign convention: PED is (near enough) always negative and YED/XED can be positive or negative — the sign of YED distinguishes normal from inferior goods, and the sign of XED distinguishes substitutes from complements.

4

Link PED directly to total revenue in any question about a firm's pricing strategy or government tax policy — raising price only raises revenue if demand is inelastic, a link many students state backwards under time pressure.

5

When analysing any change in equilibrium, always follow the four-step method in Section 18: identify the curve affected, draw the shift correctly, mark the new equilibrium, and state the effect on both price and quantity explicitly.

6

For interrelated markets questions, name the specific relationship (joint demand, competitive demand, composite demand, or joint supply) before tracing through the direction of the effect — precise terminology is usually credited separately from the analysis itself.
